title: The British Constitution
overview: The United Kingdom does not have one single written constitution. Instead, its system of government is based on laws, traditions, and principles that have developed over centuries. Together these rules form the British Constitution—a living framework that defines how the country is governed and how power is shared.

study_sections.heading: Unwritten Constitution
study_sections.content: UK has unwritten (uncodified) constitution made up of laws, customs, court decisions, and treaties. Constitution sets out how power is shared between Monarch, Parliament, and courts. Parliament is sovereign—has ultimate authority to make or change any law.

study_sections.heading: Separation of Powers
study_sections.content: Government runs country on behalf of Parliament and is accountable to it. Courts interpret and apply law; judges independent from politics. PM is Head of Government and leads Cabinet. Monarch is Head of State and performs ceremonial duties while politically neutral. Crown represents continuity of state.

study_sections.heading: Sources of Constitution
study_sections.content: Sources include: Statute law (Acts of Parliament), Common law (decisions by judges), Conventions (accepted practices), Works of authority (guides like Erskine May), Treaties (agreements with other countries).

study_sections.heading: Key Principles
study_sections.content: Separation of powers: Parliament makes laws, Government administers, courts interpret. Checks and balances prevent any branch from unlimited power. Key principles: democracy, rule of law, accountability, freedom. Constitution flexible—can evolve through new laws and reforms.

key_facts: The British Constitution is uncodified, drawing on many laws, conventions, and historical principles.
It defines how the state, citizens, and institutions relate to each other.
Powers are shared between the executive, legislature, and judiciary so no branch holds absolute authority.
The rule of law is central, ensuring that everyone, including officials, must obey the law.
The monarchy operates within constitutional conventions, acting on ministerial advice.
Sources include statute law such as the Human Rights Act 1998, Scotland Act 1998, and Equality Act 2010.
Common law developed by judges adds constitutional principles.
Conventions, like appointing a Prime Minister from the House of Commons, guide political behaviour.
Works of authority by scholars such as A.V. Dicey and Erskine May help interpret the constitution.
Royal prerogatives, now exercised by ministers, cover actions like issuing passports or declaring war.
Parliamentary sovereignty means Parliament is the ultimate legal authority.
Historic documents including the Bill of Rights 1689 and Act of Settlement 1701 underpin constitutional monarchy.
Devolution grants Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland powers over domestic matters.
The Human Rights Act allows citizens to challenge government actions in UK courts.
The Supreme Court, created in 2009, upholds constitutional principles and reviews government decisions.
The European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018 reasserted parliamentary sovereignty after Brexit.
Flexibility allows the constitution to adapt through precedent, trust, and political accountability.
memory_hook: Picture the constitution as a living framework—layers of laws, conventions, and courts flexing together to keep UK democracy upright.

options: Set of laws & traditions governing UK
questions.explanation: UK Constitution is uncodified but made of laws, customs and conventions.
Rules come from many sources, not one document.
Parliament has supreme legal authority to make laws.

options: Law-making, enforcement, judging by different bodies
questions.explanation: Prevents any one branch having too much power.
Judiciary ensures fair application of laws.

options: All: Statute law, Common law, Conventions
questions.explanation: Constitution draws from multiple sources.

options: Law developed by judges through court decisions
questions.explanation: Judge-made law is part of Constitution.
Conventions are unwritten rules guiding political behaviour.
'Erskine May' is authoritative manual on Parliament.

options: Agreements with other countries approved by Parliament
questions.explanation: International treaties form part of constitutional framework.
Monarch represents nation and performs ceremonial duties.
PM leads Cabinet and executive branch.
Parliament creates legislation.

options: Keeps courts free from political pressure
questions.explanation: Independent judges ensure fair justice.

options: Each branch can limit others' power
questions.explanation: Prevents any one institution dominating.

options: Can evolve through new laws & reforms
questions.explanation: New Acts of Parliament can update it easily.
Only Parliament can make or repeal laws.

questions.prompt: Principle ensuring no one above law?
questions.explanation: Rule of law guarantees equal treatment.
Crown represents state's enduring authority.
Awareness strengthens democracy and responsibility.
